one_line_takeaway: A breadth-first narrative survey mapping Curriculum Learning, Human-in-the-Loop RL, Active Learning, and ethics onto safe/ethical autonomous-vehicle development - useful as an organized entry point and reference table, but it contributes no new method, data, or empirical result and is not a security paper.
key_finding: The authors' synthesized conclusion is that human oversight remains essential for safe and ethical AVs and that each surveyed technique fills a distinct role: CL structures training from simple to complex tasks to improve generalization/convergence and cut training time; HITL-RL injects human reward/action/feedback to enable real-time safety intervention and build public trust; and AL reduces annotation cost and time by querying humans only on uncertain/informative instances. They argue current AVs are still years from unsupervised consumer deployment (citing Tesla, Cruise, Waymo incidents), so a hybrid human-machine approach with embedded ethical principles and accountability is required.
core_contribution: A narrative survey that organizes Human-In-The-Loop Machine Learning (HITL-ML) for autonomous vehicles (AVs, including ground vehicles, UAVs, UGVs, and AUVs) into four themes: Curriculum Learning (CL), Human-In-The-Loop Reinforcement Learning (HITL-RL, further split into reward shaping, action injection, and interactive learning), Active Learning (AL), and ethical principles. The paper's own contribution is the taxonomy/organization of ~90 cited works into comparative tables plus a set of open research directions (explainable AVs, timely human intervention, safety, security, regulatory frameworks, and reliable human input). It proposes no new algorithm, model, dataset, or experiment of its own. The stated novelty vs. prior HITL surveys is the explicit AV safety-and-ethics framing that ties CL, HITL-RL, and AL together with ethical decision-making for AVs.
threat_model: N/A - this is not a security paper and defines no threat model of its own. Security appears only tangentially: an open-directions subsection (Sec VI.D) mentions session hijacking between a vehicle and a human operator and recommends encryption, two-way authentication, and post-quantum key agreement (Ring Learning with Errors / RLWE); one surveyed AL example targets intrusion detection of unknown attacks in UAV networks (Zhang et al. [48], Open-CNN + least-confidence AL); and an open issue notes HITL involvement can improve robustness against adversarial (evasion) attacks. None of this is developed into an attacker model, and the paper's central concern is functional/ethical safety, not adversarial security.
limitations: As a narrative survey it has no reproducible search/selection methodology and no quantitative or head-to-head evaluation, so the comparative 'strengths and limitations' claims are qualitative and author-judged. Coverage is breadth-first and often one-to-two sentences per cited work, with limited critical depth. The four themes (CL, HITL-RL, AL, ethics) are treated somewhat separately rather than integrated into a unified framework. Security and adversarial-robustness are raised only as future directions without technical detail. Several 'insights' are open questions rather than evidenced findings.
